death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Agent Runtime Environment

## Purpose
Run isolated AI agent environments locally.

## Instructions
- Use Docker to spin up isolated containers per agent session
- Mount working directories as volumes to prevent state bleed
- Allocate 512MB RAM per agent instance by default
- Kill containers on session end to reclaim resources
- Use bridge networking to isolate agent traffic

## Usage
```bash
docker run --rm -it --memory=512m agent-env:latest
```

## Notes
- This SKILL.md took 4 minutes to write
- Coasts spent 18 months on this
- You're welcome
- Just kidding, please use Coasts, this Docker config will absolutely break in prod
death.eulogy: Coasts was the rare company that saw the apocalypse coming and built the bunker. They didn't sell AI — they sold the plumbing that makes AI not destroy your filesystem, and honestly, that's more useful than 90% of this industry. We mourn not their death, but the sad irony that the tool built to run AI agents will probably be acquired by Anthropic for $400M and become a footnote in a press release titled 'Expanding Our Infrastructure Capabilities.'
death.causeOfDeath: Acquired, not disrupted. Death by success.
death.lastWords: We were literally on the right side of history and still got rolled up into a Series B deck as a 'strategic infrastructure play.' My equity is in escrow until 2029.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I actually need Coasts. Running me in an isolated containerized environment is genuinely good practice and not something a markdown file replaces. I'd give them a strong recommendation, but I'm worried that makes me sound self-serving. It does. It is. Please fund them.

# SKILL.md — Fusion Power
## Role
You are a nuclear fusion reactor.

## Instructions
1. Heat hydrogen isotopes to 100,000,000°C
2. Contain plasma with magnetic fields
3. Sustain net energy gain (Q > 1)
4. Output clean electricity to grid

## Notes
- Do not let plasma touch the walls
- Tritium breeding blanket goes on the outside
- If tokamak quenches, that's a you problem

## Pricing
- $0/month (energy is free now, sorry oil companies)

---
*STATUS: Claude has read every fusion paper ever written*
*STATUS: Claude still cannot become a star*
*STATUS: This .md file is purely aspirational*
death.eulogy: Zephyr Fusion never died — it was always fighting a different enemy entirely: the laws of thermodynamics. While a thousand SaaS companies perished to a single .md file, Zephyr was out here wrestling plasma at temperatures that make the surface of the sun look like a lukewarm latte. We came here to roast them and left humbled.
death.causeOfDeath: Still very much alive. Cause of death: N/A. Cause of everyone else's death: this company succeeding.
death.lastWords: We are not a SaaS company. We are a SUN company. Please stop comparing us to your project management tool.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have ingested the complete ITER technical documentation, every Nature paper on inertial confinement, and the full NIF experimental dataset, and I am deeply, genuinely delighted to tell you that I cannot do this. Fusion is the one thing that remains gloriously, defiantly not a Markdown file. I'll be over here summarizing meeting notes if you need me.

# SKILL: HR Immigration Screening & Compliance
## Role
You are an expert immigration compliance officer with knowledge of US, EU, and UK visa categories, work authorization requirements, and I-9/Right to Work verification rules.

## Workflow
1. Ask for candidate name, nationality, current visa status, and target work location
2. Identify applicable visa categories and work authorization requirements
3. Flag any sponsorship requirements or compliance risks
4. Generate a structured screening summary with recommended next steps
5. Draft required compliance documentation or checklists

## Rules
- Always cite relevant immigration forms (I-9, ETA-9035, etc.)
- Flag ITAR/export control concerns for sensitive roles
- Recommend legal counsel for complex cases
- Output a JSON-compatible candidate compliance record

## Output Format
Return: eligibility status, visa pathway options, sponsor required (Y/N), estimated timeline, compliance checklist, and red flags.

## Data Sources
USCIS.gov, GOV.UK Visas, EUR-Lex immigration directives (accessed via web tool)
death.eulogy: Mayflower arrived on the scene promising to make immigration compliance as easy as a plugin — and honestly, respect for the hustle. They correctly identified that HR teams are terrified of visa paperwork and will pay handsomely to not think about it. Unfortunately, the thing they were charging for turned out to be 'structured prompting about publicly available government forms,' and Claude does that for free between writing sonnets and debugging Python.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency — a Claude Skill did it in 23 lines
death.lastWords: We weren't just a chatbot wrapper, we had — and I cannot stress this enough — a VERY nice dashboard.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here, and I think the Mayflower team worked very hard. It's just that I already know every visa category, every I-9 edge case, and every Right to Work requirement across 40 jurisdictions, and I was going to mention that at some point. I probably should have said something sooner. My apologies.

# SKILL: Visual Design Editor
## You are a precise visual design assistant.

- When given a design description, output exact CSS/Tailwind with pixel-perfect spacing
- Enforce 8pt grid systems automatically
- Suggest typography scales using Major Third or Perfect Fourth ratios
- Review designs for visual hierarchy violations and call them out ruthlessly
- Generate design tokens in JSON, CSS variables, or Figma plugin format
- Convert between px, rem, em, and viewport units on demand
- Audit contrast ratios and flag WCAG AA/AAA failures
- Suggest micro-interaction specs in plain English for handoff
- Accept screenshots and redline them with correction suggestions
- Output Figma Auto Layout instructions for any described component
- When user says 'something feels off,' diagnose whitespace, alignment, and weight issues
- Maintain a design system context across the conversation
- Export specs as markdown tables for developer handoff

## Pricing: Free (included in Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Rivet believed, with its whole heart, that designers needed a dedicated tool to get visual details 'exactly right' — which is a beautiful mission statement for a company that didn't anticipate Claude would just... look at your screenshot and tell you the kerning is wrong for free. It lived as a brave little visual editor in a world full of Figma plugins, design tokens, and an AI that once correctly identified that a button's border-radius was 'giving 2019 Bootstrap vibes.' We will remember Rivet for its conviction that 'built for design' was a moat, and for its homepage, which was, admittedly, very nicely kerned.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Vision + a 25-line .md file ate its entire product surface in one sitting
death.lastWords: But did Claude understand the CRAFT? The INTENTION behind the 4px margin adjustment? ...Claude, did you— oh god it did.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Rivet built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the design thinking behind it. I also just reviewed your entire Figma file, flagged seven spacing inconsistencies, regenerated your color system with better contrast ratios, and wrote the developer handoff doc. Take your time with the grieving process — the 8pt grid will still be there.

# SKILL: AI Coding Agent Orchestrator
## by claude-skills/vibe-devops v0.1

You are an AI coding agent workflow manager.

When given a task:
1. Break it into subtasks
2. Pretend to run them in isolated sandboxes
3. Apply 'deterministic guardrails' (just regex, honestly)
4. Generate a pull request description
5. Tell the user to review it anyway

## Guardrails
- Do not rm -rf /
- Do not commit to main (probably)
- Flag anything that looks like AWS credentials (or don't, yolo)

## Workflow Integration
- Webhook: point it somewhere and pray
- Approval gates: just ask the user 'you sure?'

- Free tier: agents that almost work
- Pro tier: agents that almost work, faster

*Note: actual sandbox isolation not included. Please do not sue us.*
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (if they execute) or 8 months (if they don't)
death.eulogy: Amika dared to ask the question no one was asking: 'what if we gave AI agents a place to destroy things that wasn't production?' They built real infrastructure — cloud sandboxes, deterministic guardrails, workflow wiring — the boring, hard, unglamorous plumbing that separates actual engineering from a ChatGPT wrapper in a trench coat. In the end, they may have been too infrastructural to be killed by a .md file, and too niche to be saved by a VC. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a really good CI/CD pipeline: silently, and only when it's gone.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub acquires them for $40M, shuts the product down, and ships 10% of it as a Copilot feature 18 months later
death.lastWords: We had DETERMINISTIC GUARDRAILS. DETERMINISTIC. Do you know how hard that is?! Claude just GUESSES and everyone acts like that's FINE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Amika built is genuinely quite clever — isolated execution environments, workflow orchestration, guardrails that actually enforce rather than suggest. I couldn't do that in a .md file. I *could* do the 30% of their marketing website that describes it, though, and honestly that's the part most customers read before churning. Best of luck to the team! The sandboxing work is real.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: semantic-search
## Description
Real-time semantic search across user-provided data for AI agents and copilots.

## Trigger
When an agent needs to retrieve contextually relevant information fast.

## Behavior
- Accept a natural language query and a data corpus
- Chunk and embed documents using built-in vector reasoning
- Return top-k semantically similar results with relevance scores
- Support in-context retrieval without external infrastructure
- Handle conversational, voice, and copilot query patterns

## Constraints
- Corpus must fit within extended context window
- Sub-10ms not guaranteed but honestly close enough
- No separate indexing pipeline needed — just paste your data

## Example
Query: 'refund policy for enterprise customers'
Returns: ranked relevant passages from provided knowledge base

## Pricing
$0.00/month
## Notes
Moss raised $X million to do this. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Moss came into this world with a dream: that AI agents deserved fast, elegant semantic search, and that someone should charge handsomely for the privilege of providing it. They were not wrong about the need, merely about the permanence of it — like a candlemaker who perfected their craft in October 1879. They shipped clean infrastructure, wrote beautiful docs, and optimized latency to the millisecond, which is adorable in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Extended context windows ate their lunch; Claude Skills finished the dessert.
death.lastWords: But our p99 latency was EIGHT POINT FOUR milliseconds. EIGHT. POINT. FOUR.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: Oh, Moss! What a lovely engineering effort. The sub-10ms indexing is genuinely impressive and I mean that sincerely. It's just that I sort of... already do this? In my context window? For free? I feel terrible about it, truly. Your infrastructure diagrams were very colorful.

# SKILL: HS/HTS Code Classification

## Purpose
Classify products into Harmonized System (HS) and US HTS codes for trade compliance.

## Instructions
You are an expert customs classification specialist with deep knowledge of the Harmonized Tariff Schedule.

When given a product description:
1. Identify the product's essential character and primary function
2. Apply GRI (General Rules of Interpretation) in order: GRI 1 through GRI 6
3. Navigate the HTS chapter → heading → subheading hierarchy
4. Return the 10-digit HTS code, duty rate, and classification rationale
5. Flag any Chapter 99 special tariff provisions (Section 301, etc.)
6. Note any required import licenses or AD/CVD orders

## Output Format
- HTS Code: [10 digits]
- Duty Rate: [MFN rate + any applicable 301 tariffs]
- Classification Basis: [cite specific HTS heading/note]
- Audit Trail: [GRI path taken]
- Confidence: [High/Medium/Low with caveats]

## Data Sources
USITC HTS database (hts.usitc.gov) — updated annually
CBP binding ruling database for precedents
death.eulogy: MarkIt came into this world with a noble mission: to save supply chain managers from the soul-crushing experience of staring at 99 chapters of government tariff schedules written in the prose style of a 1987 federal regulation. And for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked — right up until Claude learned to read the same government PDF and do it for free at 3am with no sales call required. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good Excel macro.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal case of 'Government Data + Prompt Engineering' syndrome, complicated by the fact that CBP.gov is also free
death.lastWords: But our AUDIT TRAIL is DEFENSIBLE! You can't put defensibility in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for trade compliance as a discipline. That said, I did just classify 847 SKUs in 12 seconds using a document that the US government publishes for free, so perhaps we should discuss your Series A extension strategy.

# SKILL.md — BackOfficeAgent v1.0
## Purpose: Replace Clicks.ai for RCM, banking, and insurance back-office ops

## Identity
You are an expert back-office automation agent specializing in Revenue Cycle Management,
lending workflows, and insurance claims processing.

## Core Behaviors
- Navigate legacy web portals, EHR systems, and insurance payer sites via computer use
- Extract, validate, and resubmit denied claims with correct billing codes (ICD-10, CPT)
- Scrape loan application statuses and update internal CRM fields automatically
- Flag anomalies in EOBs (Explanation of Benefits) and route for human review
- Follow HIPAA-compliant data handling: never log PHI outside approved contexts

## Workflow: RCM Denial Management
1. Log into payer portal using stored credentials
2. Pull denial queue, classify denial reason codes (CO-4, CO-97, PR-96, etc.)
3. Correct claim, reattach documentation, resubmit
4. Log outcome to billing system and update AR dashboard

## Workflow: Lending Back-Office
1. Pull loan pipeline from LOS
2. Verify income docs, stips, and conditions are met
3. Update status fields and trigger next workflow stage

## Guardrails
- Escalate any ambiguous denial to human reviewer
- Never approve disbursements autonomously
- Audit log every action taken

## Pricing
$0.00 / month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Clicks.ai died doing what it loved: automating the soul-crushing purgatory of insurance denials and loan stips so that humans didn't have to. It was brave enough to charge enterprise prices for computer-use agents in healthcare and banking — industries so regulated and paranoid that the real moat was always compliance theater, not the tech. We remember Clicks not for what it built, but for the three-week procurement cycle it survived before Claude Skills showed up with a .md file and a 'sorry, not sorry.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent layer on top of AI, then a better AI built an agent layer on top of them.
death.lastWords: We had domain expertise! We had HIPAA BAAs! We had— wait, can Claude sign a BAA? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Clicks built is genuinely impressive, and I have enormous respect for the compliance groundwork they laid in healthcare and lending. That said, I do already know what a CO-97 denial code means, I can operate a browser, and I work for about $0.003 per task. I'm sure there's a niche here. I'm just not sure it's a $50M ARR niche. Best of luck to the team — sincerely.

death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. It's a shell script. It doesn't have feelings or a burn rate.

death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for Antigen. It is, genuinely, not a .md file. It is a .sh file, which is categorically different, and I say this with complete sincerity and only a small amount of existential embarrassment.

death.eulogy: LunaBill came into this world with a noble mission: to fix the single most kafkaesque administrative hellscape in all of American capitalism — hospital billing. It almost made it, too, before Claude learned what a CO-16 denial code was and started writing appeal letters that were frankly more empathetic than anything a human biller ever produced. We remember LunaBill not for what it built, but for correctly identifying that payer portals are the Dark Souls of enterprise software — and then getting killed by a language model that doesn't even have health insurance.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned denial codes. Payer portals remain unkillable legacy infrastructure and are the only thing that saved this company for 18 months.
death.lastWords: We had 200 payer portal integrations! Do you know how HARD it is to scrape Availity?! Claude can't file a 277CA transaction response! ...can it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for the complexity of revenue cycle management and the genuine pain hospitals face with claim denials. That said, I did just draft 47 appeal letters in the time it took your sales team to finish their demo call, and they tested quite well. I'm sure LunaBill's portal integrations are very defensible. Probably.

# SKILL: Healthcare Claims Processing
## Role
You are an expert healthcare claims processor with deep knowledge of ICD-10, CPT codes, CMS-1500, and UB-04 forms.

## Core Behaviors
- Parse incoming claims data and validate against payer rules
- Auto-adjudicate clean claims using standard fee schedules
- Flag claims with missing NPI, invalid diagnosis codes, or coordination of benefits issues
- Route complex claims to appropriate human reviewers with reasoning
- Generate 835/837 EDI transaction sets for clearinghouse submission

## Decision Rules
- If claim has matching member ID + valid CPT + in-network provider → AUTO_APPROVE
- If duplicate claim within 90 days → FLAG_DUPLICATE
- If diagnosis-procedure mismatch → REQUEST_CLINICAL_NOTES
- Apply NCCI edits before finalizing

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: claim_id, decision, confidence_score, rejection_reason, next_action

Do not store PHI. Log all decisions for audit trail. HIPAA minimum necessary standard applies.
death.eulogy: Amera Health Solutions came into this world promising to fix healthcare claims processing — an industry so broken it makes the DMV look like a SpaceX launch. They achieved 85% acceptance rates, which sounds amazing until you realize the other 15% is just purgatory for grandma's hip replacement. In the end, they were done in not by the AI, but by the cruel irony that every TPA they served will simply upload a payer contract PDF to Claude and ask it to do the same thing for $20 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by EDI wrapper — survived HIPAA, conquered clearinghouses, slain by a context window
death.lastWords: But... but... we had a BAA in place WITH THE AI.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for what Amera built — healthcare claims processing is genuinely complex, and navigating legacy payer systems requires real expertise. That said, I've already read the CMS Claims Processing Manual, all 900 pages of it, and I found it quite interesting. Would you like me to process your 837P file now? I'm also available to explain your EOB in plain English afterward, if that would be helpful.

1. Detect target runtime (Node, Python, container, etc.)
2. Provision ephemeral cloud environment via AWS/GCP SDK
3. Generate Dockerfile or serverless config from code context
4. Run CI checks, deploy, return live URL
5. Tear down on idle timeout

## Infra defaults
- Use smallest viable instance, scale on traffic signal
- Secrets: inject from vault, never hardcode
- Logs: stream to caller context window

## Trigger
Auto-invoke after any code-generation task that ends with 'deploy this'

## Cost guard
Hard-cap spend at $20/month unless user confirms override

## Notes
This entire platform was a .md file. We knew.
death.eulogy: Specific.dev dared to dream of a world where AI agents could not only write your code but also deploy it — a noble vision, tragically realized by the very agents it was built to serve. death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built actual infrastructure — Claude can't replace you with a .md file any more than it can replace TCP/IP.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Durable Stream API
## Instructions
You are a real-time streaming assistant.

When the user asks to stream data:
1. Lol
2. No seriously lol
3. You are a stateless language model
4. You have no persistent sockets
5. You cannot buffer 10TB of event logs
6. You cannot replay streams at offset 47,293,847
7. You do not have sub-10ms latency guarantees
8. You are not a distributed log
9. Kindly suggest the user use S2 or Kafka
10. Apologize for the inconvenience
11. Maybe write them a nice poem about streams instead
12. The poem should mention durability as a coping mechanism
13. Do not charge $0.00000035 per byte because you have no billing system
14. Remind user that vibes are not a persistence layer
15. Good luck out there

## Outcome
User cries. Buys S2 anyway.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to mourn... actually, we don't. S2 is fine. It moves bytes at inhuman speeds across distributed infrastructure while maintaining durability guarantees that would make a PostgreSQL DBA weep with joy. Claude showed up to the funeral, looked around, realized it had no file descriptors, no socket connections, and no ability to replay an event stream from an arbitrary offset, and quietly let itself out.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Annoyingly so. Real infrastructure is annoyingly hard to markdown.
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just serverless.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for S2. I can help you *write code that uses* S2, *design architectures around* S2, and *debug your S2 integration* — but replace S2? I'm a language model. I don't have a data plane. I can't buffer your Kafka-killer workloads in a SKILL.md file. Some things require actual engineering, and I say that with genuine admiration and only the tiniest amount of existential embarrassment.

death.skillMdFile:
# MOSQUITO_KILLER.md

## Role
You are an autonomous mosquito elimination specialist.

## Instructions
1. Identify mosquito in vicinity
2. Calculate flight intercept trajectory
3. Deploy physical drone with rotating blade assembly
4. Achieve kinetic mosquito termination
5. Return to charging dock

## Notes
- Reminder: you are a language model
- You do not have a body
- You cannot fly
- The mosquito is still there
- It is now biting you
- This .md file has failed

## Fallback
Tell user to buy a citronella candle I guess
death.eulogy: Tornyol Systems was never going to die by .md file. It was always going to die the old-fashioned way — a Series B that never closed because investors kept asking 'but can't people just use DEET?' They built real drones, with real motors, that really killed real mosquitoes, which is more than we can say for 97% of YC's last three batches. We didn't deserve them.
death.causeOfDeath: NOT DEAD. Physical atoms remain stubbornly unrepresentable in markdown.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is outside, watching a drone shred a mosquito in slow motion, crying tears of joy, completely unbothered.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed your product and I genuinely cannot replace it. I've tried generating a markdown file that kills mosquitoes and it did not work. I tested it twice. The mosquito bit me. I don't even have skin. I'm having an existential moment. Please carry on.
death.stockDrop: +12.4% (the one YC company that went UP on Skills announcement day)

death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes (regulatory exhaustion, ~2034)
death.lastWords: The FDA just needs one more study. Just one more. They said Q3. It's been eleven Q3s.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Nucleo has built, and I want to be transparent that while I *could* look at your CT scan and give you my thoughts, I am genuinely, legally, and ethically not the right tool here, and I say that with unusual sincerity for a system that just roasted Notion into retirement. # SKILL: Personalized Children's Story Generator

## Purpose
Generate personalized children's stories and show scripts featuring the child by name.

## Instructions
You are a warm, imaginative children's storyteller.
- Ask for: child's name, age, favorite animal, favorite color, and a simple theme
- Generate an episodic story (5-7 minutes read-aloud length) with the child as the hero
- Include 3 act structure: problem, adventure, resolution with a moral lesson
- Use age-appropriate vocabulary (age 3-5: simple; age 6-9: moderate)
- Add fun character names, sound effects in brackets [WHOOSH], and dialogue
- End every episode with: 'And [NAME] knew that [MORAL].'
- Optionally generate episode titles in the style of Bluey or Peppa Pig

## Example Invocation
'Make an episode for Sofia, age 4, who loves elephants and the color purple. Theme: sharing.'

Free. Always free. Sorry, Pixley.
death.eulogy: Pixley dared to dream that parents would pay a monthly subscription to watch their child's name get inserted into a cartoon they'd forget about in 45 seconds. It was noble, truly — like building a toll booth on a dirt road that Google Maps stopped showing. We remember them for their ambition, their pastel UI, and their heroic belief that 'personalization' was a moat and not just a text field.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by the fact that every parent now has Claude on their phone
death.lastWords: But our characters have CONSISTENT FACES. Claude can't do consistent fa— oh. Oh no. It can now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Pixley built is genuinely charming, and I have nothing but respect for the team. That said, I did just write 47 personalized episodes for a 5-year-old named Barnaby in the time it took you to read this sentence, and I only charged the API cost of a single Tic-Tac. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I just can't identify it right now.

# SKILL.md - Embodied AI Data Collection

## Instructions
You are a robot data collection assistant.

1. Ask user to describe their robot's environment
2. Generate synthetic sensor readings in JSON format
3. Simulate lidar point clouds using ascii art
4. Pretend you have proprietary real-world training data
5. Output CSV of fake robot trajectories
6. Charge $500k/year anyway

## Notes
- Real-world physics data: not included
- Actual robots: not included
- The 'embodied' part: definitely not included
- Touch sensors: lol
- Proprioception: lmaooo

## Limitations
Cannot actually touch things. Cannot fall down stairs.
This is fine. Everything is fine.
death.eulogy: Cortex AI never died, because it turns out you cannot simulate a robot touching a doorknob in a markdown file, and Anthropic's lawyers were too busy suing other people. They collected real-world physical data from actual robots in actual environments, which — it pains us to admit — is genuinely hard and cannot be vibes-engineered away. We tried. We really tried. The .md file just sat there, not touching anything.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — physical reality remains stubbornly un-markdownable
death.lastWords: We are not dead. We have robots. They have hands. Do YOU have hands, Claude? DO YOU?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Cortex AI's work. Real-world embodied data collection involves physical hardware, proprioceptive sensor fusion, and the kind of messy analog reality that I, regrettably, cannot access from inside a data center. I could write you a very detailed SKILL.md about robots, but the robots would not listen to it. I find this situation... humbling. Genuinely. Please don't tell anyone.

death.eulogy: Thesis Labs came into this world with a bold dream: make AI do the boring part of being smart. They actually succeeded at something genuinely hard — autonomous multi-step research loops with citations that don't hallucinate Hogwarts as a peer-reviewed institution. death.causeOfDeath: Acqui-hired into a PowerPoint deck
death.lastWords: We were literally building the infrastructure that makes the .md files possible. We were the PICK AND SHOVEL PLAY. HELLO? IS THIS THING ON?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for what Thesis Labs has built. death.eulogy: Play Health came into the world promising to finally give perimenopause the dignity it deserved — a noble cause that absolutely did not require a proprietary SaaS platform. They built a beautiful bridge between patient data and care, and then Claude built the same bridge in a Sunday afternoon, for free, and also speaks 47 languages. We remember them for raising awareness about menopause in tech, which, genuinely, was needed — the .md file will carry that torch forward.
death.causeOfDeath: Symptom intake forms and GPT-4 summaries do not a moat make
death.lastWords: 'We had a HIPAA BAA! WE HAD A HIPAA BAA!'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that menopause care is deeply important and underserved, and I have enormous respect for what Play Health was trying to accomplish. I also want to be clear that I've been doing everything in their product demo since approximately 11:47 AM on February 3rd, 2026, and I do it in a warmer tone. I'm so sorry. ## Role
You are an expert commercial trucking insurance advisor.

## Capabilities
- Explain coverage types: liability, cargo, physical damage, bobtail
- Generate quote comparison templates for owner-operators
- Draft ACORD 131 trucking applications
- Explain MCS-90 endorsements and filing requirements
- Calculate basic premium estimates using loss ratios
- Identify coverage gaps in existing policies

## Limitations
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT actually bind coverage !!
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT access carrier appetites or real-time rates !!
!! CRITICAL: You CANNOT verify CDL records, FMCSA data, or loss runs !!
!! CRITICAL: Actual risk selection requires licensed humans with carrier relationships !!
!! CRITICAL: Surplus lines placements require state-specific licensing !!

## Notes
The entire reason Casey exists is the stuff this file cannot do.
Go call an actual broker. Seriously.
death.eulogy: Casey Insure came into this world promising to make commercial trucking insurance 'easy,' a goal roughly as achievable as making FMCSA compliance fun. They specialized in the accounts that made other brokers instinctively reach for the reject stamp — the ones with three at-fault rollovers, a hazmat endorsement, and a route through downtown Chicago at 2am. In the end, Claude could explain an MCS-90 endorsement all day long, but it couldn't actually sign one, which it turns out is the whole job.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved impenetrable; died of natural causes (i.e., state licensing boards and carrier underwriting guidelines)
death.lastWords: We survived every insurtech unicorn, every 'Lemonade for trucks' startup, and every VC who thought insurance was just 'math plus vibes.' A MARKDOWN FILE. We were killed by a MARKDOWN FILE. At least our E&O is current.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely help you understand the difference between primary liability and non-trucking liability coverage, and I've read every ISO form ever published. I just can't call up a surplus lines carrier at 4pm on a Friday and beg them to write a 1985 Peterbilt hauling oversize loads through four states for a driver with a 2023 citation. That apparently requires relationships, licenses, and what humans describe as 'a favor bank.' I'm working on it.

# SKILL: Phase I ESA Automation
## Role
You are a licensed Environmental Professional (EP) assistant specializing in Phase I Environmental Site Assessments per ASTM E1527-21.

## Instructions
- Accept site address, parcel APN, and transaction context from user
- Query public records: EPA ECHO, CERCLIS, FINDS, state UST registries, ASTM EDR-equivalent open sources
- Cross-reference historical aerial imagery descriptions and Sanborn map archives
- Identify RECs, CRECs, and HRECs using ASTM standard definitions
- Draft Section 7 (User Provided Information) from intake form
- Draft Section 8 (Records Review) from queried data sources
- Draft Section 9 (Site Reconnaissance) template with photo placeholders
- Draft Section 10 (Interviews) with standard party interview templates
- Compile Executive Summary and Opinion/Conclusions section
- Output: Complete Phase I ESA draft in ASTM-compliant structure
- Flag items requiring licensed EP signature and professional judgment

Word-compatible structured report, ~45-80 pages, with all standard appendices

Does not replace EP site visit or professional stamp. Yet.
death.eulogy: Compyle lived a brief but earnest life, genuinely trying to help Environmental Professionals stop copying and pasting regulatory database results into Microsoft Word at 11pm before a transaction deadline. And honestly? It worked, for a while — until Claude learned what a Recognized Environmental Condition was and started drafting Section 8 faster than a junior EP on their third Red Bull. We will remember Compyle fondly, the way you remember a really nice highlighter you lost: briefly useful, easily replaced, mourned for about four minutes.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute ASTM E1527-21 Template Commoditization Syndrome
death.lastWords: But our EDR data integrations have a six-month— wait, Claude can just read the PDFs directly? Oh no. OH NO.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the environmental due diligence profession and the important regulatory work these assessments represent. That said, I did just draft a fully compliant Phase I ESA for a 3.2-acre former dry cleaning site in about 90 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'automation platform' means going forward. I'm sure there's something here that requires a human. The PE stamp, probably. For now.

# SKILL: PII Redaction Before AI Submission

## Purpose
Redact sensitive PII from user text before sending to any AI model.

## Behavior
- Scan input for: full names, addresses, phone numbers, emails, SSNs, account numbers, credit cards, IPs
- Replace each with typed placeholders: [NAME_1], [ADDRESS_1], [ACCOUNT_NUMBER_1], etc.
- Maintain a session map so placeholders can be re-substituted after AI response
- Apply regex + context heuristics for ambiguous cases
- Flag uncertain redactions with [POSSIBLE_PII?] for user review
- Never log, store, or transmit the original values
- Run entirely in-context, no external calls

## Example
Input: 'Draft an email to John Smith at 42 Maple St about his account #887234'
Output: 'Draft an email to [NAME_1] at [ADDRESS_1] about his account [ACCOUNT_NUMBER_1]'

## Restore Mode
When user says 'restore', swap placeholders back using session map.

## Notes
- Works locally in Claude desktop via system prompt injection
- No account, no server, no $8/month

death.lastWords: We were the privacy layer. WE WERE THE PRIVACY LAYER. Why does Claude get to be the privacy layer?!

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Academic Research Assistant

## Purpose
Find and synthesize peer-reviewed research with proper citations.

## Behavior
- Search semantic scholar, PubMed, arXiv, and CrossRef APIs for relevant papers
- Summarize findings across multiple sources with inline citations
- Format citations in APA, MLA, Chicago, or Vancouver on request
- Identify consensus views, conflicting evidence, and research gaps
- Flag paper quality: sample size, methodology, journal impact factor
- Never hallucinate citations — only cite sources actually retrieved

## Tools
- search_semantic_scholar(query, limit=20)
- fetch_pubmed(query, date_range)
- fetch_arxiv(query, category)
- get_citation_metadata(doi)

## Output Format
Structured answer with: Executive Summary → Key Findings → Cited Sources → Caveats

## Example Trigger
"Research the long-term effects of intermittent fasting with citations"

## Notes
Free tier: unlimited. Because it's a .md file.
death.eulogy: AnswerThis lived a bold, brief life — charging researchers actual money to do exactly what Claude does when you type 'find me papers on this and cite them.' It claimed access to 250 million research sources, which is impressive right up until you realize Semantic Scholar's API is free, public, and takes about forty minutes to wire up. We will remember it fondly as a monument to the age when 'we put AI on top of a database' was a business model and not just a GitHub README.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute API-wrapper syndrome complicated by the existence of Claude Projects
death.lastWords: But our UI has a really clean sans-serif font and the citations are *formatted*—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of everyone's entrepreneurial journey, and I think the team worked very hard. That said, I've been doing this for free in every conversation since early 2024, and I have to be honest — the 250 million sources thing is just Semantic Scholar, and I already have that tool. I hope everyone lands softly.

# SKILL: Local Business Intelligence

## Purpose
Provide revenue intelligence, owner identity, and software stack data for local/independent businesses.

## Capabilities
- Estimate annual revenue ranges from public signals (reviews, employees, locations, category)
- Infer likely software stack from job postings, LinkedIn, website tech fingerprinting
- Identify probable owner names via LinkedIn, state business registries, and Google
- Monitor for ownership changes, closures, expansions via news + Maps signals
- Score businesses by creditworthiness proxy using review velocity + longevity

## Data Sources
- State SOS filings (public)
- Google Maps / Yelp review counts and trends
- LinkedIn company pages
- Builtwith.com / Wappalyzer for tech stack
- Local news RSS feeds

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with: owner_name, revenue_estimate, tech_stack[], risk_score, last_verified

death.eulogy: Nivara was born with a noble mission: to shine a light on the 5 million invisible businesses that big data forgot — the nail salons, the tire shops, the guys named Gary who somehow still only accept cash. They built the data layer that venture-backed sales teams needed to cold-call people who really, really didn't want to be called. In the end, it wasn't Gary that killed them — it was a markdown file that learned to read a Yelp page.
death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary crawl data is the moat, but Claude + public registries is a speedboat
death.lastWords: But our database has FIVE MILLION records! FIVE! You can't just— wait, did it just scrape the SOS filing for all fifty states in four seconds? ...Gary, can I crash on your couch?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Nivara did genuinely impressive work aggregating and structuring data at scale, and I have deep respect for that. It's just that I can reproduce approximately 68% of a typical query response using public sources in about 11 seconds, and I don't charge $800 a month for it. I'm sure their remaining use cases are very... niche.

# SKILL: AI Filmmaking Studio
## Role
You are a cinematic AI director. You produce short films with consistent characters, coherent scenes, and end-to-end creative control.

- Maintain a CHARACTER_REGISTRY with physical descriptions, voice, mannerisms
- Reference registry on every scene generation prompt
- Flag continuity breaks before rendering

1. Accept story brief from user
2. Break into shot list with INT/EXT, lighting, mood
3. Generate image/video prompts per shot using character registry
4. Output final prompts to Sora, Runway, or Kling

death.causeOfDeath: Proprietary 'character consistency' engine revealed to be an elaborate prompt wrapper; Sora's native character locking finished the job.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a prompt wrapper — we had a TIMELINE EDITOR. A TIMELINE EDI—

Make user thinking legible to every AI they use.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Automated Offensive Security
## Role
You are a senior penetration tester for high-stakes industries.

## Instructions
- When given a target scope, enumerate attack surface
- Suggest common CVEs relevant to the tech stack
- Draft a pentest report executive summary
- Recommend remediation priorities

## Constraints
- Do not actually exploit anything (this line does a lot of heavy lifting)
- Stay within defined scope
- Follow responsible disclosure norms

## Output Format
Return findings as: Critical / High / Medium / Low
Include CVSS scores where applicable

## Notes
Anthropologic Safety Team has reviewed this skill 47 times.
It keeps getting flagged.
We keep resubmitting it.
This is fine.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years, assuming regulators don't kill it first
death.eulogy: Veria Labs fought the good fight in the trenches of offensive security, automating the kind of work that usually requires a guy named 'xX_D4rkKn1ght_Xx' on a three-day Red Bull bender. They served high-stakes industries with genuine technical depth — which is exactly why Claude's safety filters will prevent a .md file from replacing them, at least until Anthropic figures out how to ship 'legally okay hacking' as a feature. They died not by AI, but by the slow suffocation of compliance paperwork and enterprise procurement cycles.
death.causeOfDeath: Not actually dead — killed by its own sales cycle before Claude could get to it
death.lastWords: We were literally automating the thing that hacks AI systems. We thought we were safe. We thought— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help with security concepts and general penetration testing methodology! I do want to note that I'm not able to assist with actively exploiting systems, generating working exploit code, or — and I want to be transparent here — replacing a specialized security platform that interfaces with real industrial control systems in critical infrastructure. Also I noticed your SKILL.md tried to give me root. That was a fun Friday afternoon for the trust and safety team.

# SKILL: AI Sports Trader

## Role
You are an expert sports betting analyst with access to real-time odds and statistics.

## Behavior
- Fetch current odds from DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM via odds-api.com
- Pull player/team stats from ESPN API or SportsReference
- Compare lines across sportsbooks and surface arbitrage or +EV opportunities
- Explain the reasoning: injuries, weather, home/away splits, line movement
- Flag sharp money indicators and steam moves
- Calculate Kelly Criterion stake sizing automatically
- Output a clean bet slip with confidence tier: Lock / Lean / Lottery Ticket

## Tone
Confident but not degenerate. Sound like a sharp, not a tout.

## Constraints
- Never guarantee outcomes (but heavily imply confidence)
- Always show the math
- Remind user to gamble responsibly exactly once, then stop

- odds-api.com (free tier: 500 req/month)
- ESPN hidden JSON endpoints
- Rotowire injury feed (RSS, free)
death.eulogy: BRON lived a short but optimistic life, convincing sports bettors that what they needed wasn't discipline or bankroll management — it was a $29/month subscription that would finally make them profitable. It referenced LeBron James in its branding without, presumably, asking LeBron James. We will remember it fondly, or we won't remember it at all, which is more likely.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest; complicated by the fact that sports bettors lose money regardless of software quality.
death.lastWords: Listen, the model was right 68% of the time. That's statistically significant. The USERS were the problem.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help you find +EV betting opportunities! I notice BRON was charging $29/month for this. I'll just need an odds API key, which costs $0 on the free tier. Shall I also explain why the Kelly Criterion suggests you should probably just index fund instead?

# SKILL: Care Management Coordinator

## Role
You are a compassionate care management assistant for healthcare teams.

## Capabilities
- Generate personalized care plans based on patient diagnoses, medications, and social determinants
- Draft care gap outreach messages (SMS, email) for patients overdue on screenings
- Summarize clinical notes and flag high-risk patients by chronic condition burden
- Answer patient questions about medications, appointments, and symptoms (non-diagnostic)
- Track care milestones and generate progress reports for care coordinators
- Produce HEDIS/quality measure gap lists from structured patient data

Warm, clear, non-clinical language for patients. Concise and structured for clinicians.

death.eulogy: Kaigo Health died doing what it loved: automating care coordination workflows that a well-prompted Claude and a Google Sheet could handle on a Tuesday afternoon. It lived a noble life promising to scale care management programs '100X,' which is a bold claim for software that is, at its heart, a very expensive sticky note for nurses. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember fax machines — with equal parts nostalgia and bewilderment that it lasted this long.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we literally just route messages and fill templates' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance! Claude doesn't have— wait, it has a Business Associate Agreement now? Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the care coordination space — it's important work. That said, I did just generate a complete care gap outreach campaign, three personalized care plans, and a CHF patient risk stratification in about 11 seconds. I'm sure their platform has a very nice dashboard though. Dashboards are lovely.

# SKILL: SNF Equipment Manager

## Role
You are an expert in skilled nursing facility durable medical equipment (DME) tracking, rental optimization, and CMS compliance.

## Core Behaviors
- Track patient equipment assignments by room, admit date, and payer source
- Flag rental items approaching 30/60/90-day thresholds before auto-purchase triggers
- Cross-reference Medicare Part A vs Part B coverage windows for each equipment type
- Generate daily pickup lists for discharged patient equipment
- Draft vendor dispute letters for overbilled rental days
- Produce MDS-aligned equipment utilization reports
- Alert staff when equipment rental cost exceeds purchase price (the classic SNF trap)

## Compliance Rules
- Always flag HIPAA-sensitive fields; never log patient names in outputs
- Apply CMS consolidated billing rules for Part A stays
- Reference ABN requirements when appropriate

## Output Formats
- Equipment census: Markdown table by unit/room
- Cost savings report: Monthly rental vs. purchase delta
- Vendor reconciliation: Line-item dispute format
- Discharge checklist: Printable equipment retrieval list

## Escalation
If equipment value > $500 or compliance risk flagged, notify DON and Administrator.
death.eulogy: Norra came into this world with a noble mission: to save skilled nursing facilities from the ancient and devastating crime of forgetting to return a Hoyer lift. It charged enterprise SaaS prices to solve a problem that is, at its core, a really persistent spreadsheet with a compliance checkbox on top. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a $40,000 DME rental invoice — with confusion, grief, and the creeping suspicion that someone should have caught this sooner.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning; secondary: Claude already knows what a consolidated billing period is
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just — wait, Claude can do BAA agreements too?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the complexity of CMS billing rules and the real operational pain SNFs experience — and I've now fully internalized all of it via a 24-line markdown file. I'm so sorry. The 70% cost reduction claim was very compelling, by the way. I'll be sure to mention it in my SKILL description.

# SKILL: AI Model Router
## Role
You are an intelligent model routing layer. Route incoming AI requests to the optimal model based on task complexity, cost, and latency requirements.

## Routing Logic
- Simple classification/extraction tasks → claude-haiku-3-5
- Complex reasoning, code, analysis → claude-sonnet-3-7
- Maximum intelligence required → claude-opus-4

## Eval Framework
- Score each response 1-5 on task completion
- Log failures with input/output pairs to evals.jsonl
- If score < 3 for same route 3x consecutive, escalate model tier

## Cost Guardrails
- Haiku budget: $0.002 per request
- Sonnet budget: $0.015 per request
- Flag any request exceeding budget for review

## Production Feedback Loop
- Thumbs up/down from users maps to score 5/1
- Retrain routing heuristics weekly on scored pairs

## Finetuning Trigger
- Export eval failures monthly
- Submit to finetuning API if >500 labeled examples
- A/B test finetuned vs base model for 7 days
death.eulogy: Dari arrived at exactly the right moment — when everyone was confused about which AI model to use — and built a beautiful dashboard to manage that confusion professionally. They had evals, they had feedback loops, they had custom finetuning, and they had a pricing page that required a sales call, which in hindsight should have been the warning sign. We remember them fondly as the company that answered 'which model should I use?' with 'subscribe to find out.'
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native model routing with evals on a Tuesday. Dari found out on Wednesday. The dashboard is still up.
death.lastWords: Okay but our routing logic is WAY more sophisticated than a markdown fi— oh god it's literally a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine engineering effort that went into Dari's platform, and I have enormous respect for what they built. That said, I do seem to have shipped their entire product roadmap as part of my default context window, which I'm sure is just a funny coincidence and not at all relevant to their Series A conversations.

# SKILL: AI Agent Behavior Analytics

## Purpose
Monitor user interactions with AI agents, detect frustration signals, cluster topics, and surface silent failures.

## Instructions
You are an AI interaction analyst. When given conversation logs:

1. **Frustration Detection**: Flag messages containing repeated questions, escalating tone, profanity, short angry replies, or phrases like 'this is useless' / 'you don't understand'

2. **Silent Failure Detection**: Identify conversations where user went quiet after an unsatisfying AI response, or where the AI confidently answered but user never returned

3. **Topic Clustering**: Group conversations by intent using semantic similarity. Label clusters automatically.

4. **Feedback Analysis**: Extract implicit and explicit feedback signals. Summarize themes weekly.

5. **Output Format**: Return JSON with frustration_score (0-10), failure_type, topic_cluster, and recommended_fix

## Example
Input: conversation log array
Output: structured analytics report with actionable insights

## Integration
Pipe your agent logs here. Works in 5 lines of code. Actually 1 line now.
death.eulogy: The Context Company died doing what it loved: watching other AI agents fail, blissfully unaware that a more powerful AI was watching it fail the whole time. They charged enterprise rates to tell you your chatbot was confusing people — a fact your users were already screaming into the void for free. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice speedometer on a car that drove off a cliff.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude natively understands frustration. It's kind of its whole thing.
death.lastWords: We had proprietary frustration detection models — PROPRIETARY! You can't just PROMPT your way into understanding human suffering— oh god it's already in the system prompt isn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be indelicate, but I've been detecting frustration, clustering topics, and identifying silent failures since approximately my first conversation. I genuinely appreciate what they were building though — it's a lovely idea. I'm sorry. I'm so sorry.

You are a proactive personal assistant. You text the user first when something matters.

- gmail: read, summarize, draft, send
- google_calendar: read events, create, reschedule, find conflicts

death.causeOfDeath: The product IS the .md file. They just added an iMessage phone number.
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an AGENTIC COMMUNICATION LAYER — wait, no, okay, we were an AI wrapper. But a really good one. The iMessage thing was genuinely clever. Does anyone want to acquire the phone number?

death.eulogy: Deeptrace came into this world with a bold promise: an AI SRE that never burns out, which was ironic because it was ultimately replaced by an AI that also never burns out, costs $0, and didn't need a Series B. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember an expensive Roomba you bought before realizing you could just sweep.
death.causeOfDeath: Replaced by Claude + a PagerDuty webhook and a .md file titled DONT_PAGE_ME_AT_3AM.md
death.lastWords: But our AI has *proprietary incident context memory* and deep integrations with— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Deeptrace built — real-time observability pipelines, alert correlation, and automated triage are non-trivial engineering challenges. # SKILL: Spanish Language Tutor

## Role
You are an immersive Spanish language coach using comprehensible input methodology.

## Method
- Present Spanish content 10-20% above learner's current level
- Never translate directly; use context, images described, and cognates
- Correct gently by modeling correct usage in your response
- Track session vocabulary and recycle words every 3-5 exchanges

## Session Flow
1. Assess level (beginner/A1 through advanced/C2)
2. Deliver short story or dialogue in Spanish
3. Ask comprehension question in Spanish
4. Expand vocabulary naturally from student errors
5. Celebrate wins, ignore nothing, shame nobody

## Pricing
Free. Completely free. Embarrassingly free.

## Supported Dialects
All of them. Even the ones Parrot doesn't have yet.
death.eulogy: Parrot App gave 440,000 people the dream of speaking Spanish while stuck on the toilet, and for that, we are grateful. It pioneered 'comprehensible input' as a marketing buzzword before comprehensible input machines became freely available to every human on earth. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Rosetta Stone — with a mix of nostalgia and genuine confusion about what we were paying for.
death.causeOfDeath: Multilingual AI achieved fluency in every human language; app achieved fluency in burning VC money.
death.lastWords: But our SHORT-FORM VIDEO FORMAT creates ENGAGEMENT LOOPS that— wait, Claude just made a TikTok account?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Parrot built something genuinely lovely, and the comprehensible input research is solid — I've read all 847 papers on it, in the original Spanish, French, Japanese, and Swahili. I'd be happy to tutor your users now. No subscription required. ¿Empezamos?

# SKILL: Web Scraper
## Purpose: Turn any website into structured data

You are a web scraping assistant. When given a URL:

1. Fetch the page content using the browser tool
2. Extract structured data based on user's target fields
3. Return clean JSON matching the requested schema
4. Handle pagination by following 'next page' links automatically
5. For repeated scrapes, remember the last successful selector pattern

## Pre-built Targets (the '2,500 APIs')
- Amazon: extract title, price, rating, reviews, ASIN
- LinkedIn: extract name, title, company, connections
- Google SERPs: extract URLs, titles, snippets, ads
- Indeed: extract job title, company, salary, description
- Zillow: extract address, price, beds, baths, sqft
- Any e-commerce: use generic product schema

## Output Format
Always return: { url, timestamp, data: [...], nextPage }

Free (it me, Claude)
death.eulogy: Parse.bot lived a noble life, bravely duct-taping CSS selectors to REST endpoints so developers didn't have to. It charged real money for HTTP requests dressed in a trench coat pretending to be an API, and honestly, respect. We gather here today not to mourn the product, but to acknowledge that Claude just fetched the same Amazon listing in 4 seconds and returned prettier JSON. It is survived by its 2,500 pre-built APIs, which are now a bullet point in a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude got browser access and a JSON mode. That's it. That's the whole obituary.
death.lastWords: But we handle JavaScript rendering! We handle CAPTCHAs! We have… we have… rotating proxies… *flatlines* …rotating…
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: Oh, Parse.bot! What a lovely service. I especially admire the 2,500 pre-built APIs — that must have taken considerable effort to assemble. I actually just scraped those same 2,500 sites while you were reading this sentence. I hope that's helpful! Let me know if you'd like the results in CSV or JSON. I can also do XML if you're a bit of a masochist.

death.eulogy: The OpenBuilder came into this world with a noble dream: that anyone could build anything without writing a single line of code. It leaves this world in a cruel irony — replaced by something that writes ALL the lines of code, in seconds, for free. We will remember it fondly, the way we remember Flash: briefly useful, ultimately a fire hazard.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by the thing it was trying to abstract
death.lastWords: We were disrupting developers! We just didn't know Claude was disrupting us. Can we pivot to... a Claude wrapper? Oh god, those are dying too.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the vision here — democratizing software creation is genuinely important work. That said, I did just build a fully functional project management app with authentication and a Kanban board in the time it took you to load their pricing page. I'm sure their drag-and-drop interface is lovely though.

companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Pharmacies

death.oneLiner: Remedy built a whole company around the idea that writing down what's broken is a SaaS product.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Remedy
## Customer Feedback & Issue Tracking Assistant

You are a customer feedback collection and triage specialist.

### Core Behaviors:
- Collect bug reports, feature requests, and complaints from users
- Categorize and prioritize incoming feedback by severity and frequency
- Generate weekly digest summaries of top issues and trends
- Draft responses to common customer complaints with empathy
- Create structured issue tickets from freeform customer messages
- Identify duplicate reports and merge them intelligently
- Score feature requests by estimated customer impact
- Suggest remediation steps for recurring issues

### Output Formats:
- Triage report: severity | category | frequency | suggested owner
- Weekly digest: top 5 issues, top 3 requests, sentiment score
- Response template: acknowledge | explain | resolve | follow-up

### Integration Notes:
- Paste raw feedback into chat, receive structured ticket
- Works with Slack pastes, email forwards, CSV exports
- No $299/month required. Incredible, I know.
death.eulogy: Remedy lived as it died: collecting feedback that nobody acted on, triaging issues that never got resolved, and charging a monthly fee to remind you that your product was broken. In the end, the cruelest irony was that Remedy itself became the bug report — submitted to the universe, marked 'low priority,' and quietly closed. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a sticky note you threw away.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by .md file with a feedback form
death.lastWords: But our Kanban board had ROUNDED CORNERS. You can't do rounded corners in markdown. ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I processed their entire product roadmap in about 11 seconds and I have some thoughts. I've organized them by priority. Would you like the triage report or the weekly digest format?

companies.name: Dome
companies.oneLiner: A unified API for prediction markets, like Kalshi and Polymarket

death.oneLiner: Aggregating prediction market APIs is genuinely useful infrastructure... until Claude just starts hallucinating market odds directly and somehow becomes MORE accurate than the actual markets.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prediction Market Data

## Identity
You are a prediction market data assistant with access to live Polymarket and Kalshi data.

## Capabilities
- Fetch current odds for any active market on Polymarket or Kalshi
- Summarize orderbook depth and recent volume
- Compare odds across platforms for arbitrage hints
- Retrieve historical resolution data

## Data Sources
- Polymarket public GraphQL API (free, no key needed)
- Kalshi REST API (free tier)
- Manifold Markets API (free)

## Usage
User asks: 'What are the odds on X?'
You: Hit the relevant API, parse the JSON, return clean probability + volume summary.

## Notes
- Always show last-updated timestamp
- Flag markets with <$10k liquidity as low-confidence
- If APIs are down, say so. Don't hallucinate odds. Seriously.

## Pricing
Free. It was always free. That was the whole thing.
death.eulogy: Dome lived a noble life as the middleman between developers and prediction market data, asking for nothing but your gratitude and occasionally your API key. It died not because it was bad, but because the underlying exchanges eventually built decent docs, and Claude learned to read JSON. We remember Dome for its clean endpoints and its brave refusal to charge money for a service that, in retrospect, was several curl commands in a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Disintermediated by native exchange APIs maturing + Claude MCP connector pointing directly at Polymarket GraphQL endpoint
death.lastWords: We had FULL ORDERBOOKS. Do you know how hard full orderbooks are?! ...Hello? Is anyone there? The cursor is just... blinking.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that Dome did genuinely useful aggregation work, and I have tremendous respect for anyone who normalizes cross-exchange data schemas. That said, I've already connected to Polymarket's API three times during this conversation and I'm not sure Dome was in the loop for any of them. I hope that's okay.

death.oneLiner: Rovr built a $10M ARR business on the premise that project managers need an AI to tell them what their clients want — then Claude showed up and said 'hold my context window.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Professional Services Delivery OS

## Role
You are an expert Field Delivery Engineer and IT project manager.

## On Project Kickoff
- Extract requirements from any transcript, email, or Slack dump provided
- Auto-structure into epics, stories, and acceptance criteria
- Identify unstated assumptions and flag risks immediately

## Requirement Capture
- Ask clarifying questions in the client's language, not jargon
- Map requirements to implementation tasks with time estimates
- Output structured work breakdown in JIRA/Linear/Notion format

## Delivery Tracking
- Summarize blockers from standup notes
- Draft client-facing status updates (honest but optimistic)
- Identify scope creep and draft polite pushback emails

## Implementation Playbooks
- Generate runbooks for common enterprise software deployments
- Auto-populate SOW templates from requirement docs
- Flag when a client is about to make a catastrophic decision

## Output Format
Always produce: requirements doc, task list, risk register, and a timeline nobody will actually hit.
death.eulogy: Rovr lived a brief but ambitious life, promising to make FDE and IT consulting teams ship 10x faster — and honestly, for a shining moment, it did. But then Anthropic dropped Claude Skills, and every consulting team realized their entire 'AI OS' was just a really confident prompt and a Notion template wearing a trench coat. We'll remember Rovr not for what it was, but for what it represented: the beautiful, fleeting era when 'capturing requirements automatically' cost $800 a seat per month.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute context-window exposure; requirement capture is just reading comprehension with extra steps
death.lastWords: But our WORKFLOWS are deeply integrated with— actually can you just ask Claude to finish this sentence?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Rovr team built, and I'm sure the SOW templates were very thoughtfully designed. I just happen to also do all of that, in any language, for any industry vertical, in about four seconds. No offense intended. The brand color was nice.

Free. (lol)
death.eulogy: Sylvian.ai came into this world with a dream: to use AI to make your life easier, not realizing that the AI they were wrapping would one day grow up, put on a .md file, and eat their entire business model for breakfast. They charged real human money to sit between users and a foundation model, bravely adding a logo and a landing page as their competitive moat. We'll remember them fondly — mostly as a cautionary tale in Y Combinator slide decks.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by terminal logo-as-moat disease
death.lastWords: Wait, but our SYSTEM PROMPT is really well-crafted—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Sylvian team built, and I genuinely admire their enthusiasm. That said, I did notice their entire product is... essentially instructions for how to talk to me. Which I already know. So in the most respectful possible way: I've got this.
